target.title: fix(codex): recover context overflow and budget skip paths
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes two tightly related Codex native-session recovery edges found while replaying a Discord-shaped session after the 5.27 upgrade:

1. If a resumed Codex app-server turn accepts `turn/start` but later terminally fails with a context-window overflow while the context engine owns compaction, clear the stale app-server thread binding so the outer retry starts a fresh native Codex thread.
2. If the CLI budget-compaction lifecycle receives Codex app-server's intentional automatic-compaction skip (`codex app-server owns automatic compaction`), treat that as a non-fatal skip instead of failing a successful turn after the model already responded.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Codex native app-server sessions recover from a terminal context-window overflow on a resumed context-engine thread by clearing the stale thread binding, and CLI budget compaction treats Codex app-server's intentional automatic-compaction skip as non-fatal after a successful turn.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout installed into the gateway-resolved global OpenClaw prefix, Node 24, Codex app-server runtime, local gateway health endpoint, and a Discord-shaped agent route. Channel/session identifiers were anonymized.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm build
npm install -g .
<gateway-openclaw> --version
curl -sS -m 5 http://127.0.0.1:18789/health
openclaw agent --session-key <discord-session> --channel discord --message "[exact-route-smoke] Reply with exactly: OK" --timeout 900 --json
```

- Evidence after fix: The local runtime reported `OpenClaw 2026.5.28 (3fd6cc0)`, the gateway health endpoint returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}`, the exact-route smoke returned `status=ok`, `payload.text="OK"`, and `exitCode=0`, and the anonymized logs contained `skipping codex app-server compaction for non-manual trigger` without the prior fatal compaction failure.
- Observed result after fix: The replay started a fresh native Codex thread after the stale resumed thread overflowed, subsequent turns resumed that new thread, the old `Auto-compaction could not recover this turn` message did not recur, and the successful assistant response was preserved instead of being converted into a CLI failure.
- What was not tested: Live Discord delivery with real channel IDs was not repeated in the PR body evidence; local identifiers, channel IDs, session IDs, auth profile IDs, hostnames, and absolute paths were redacted.

## Before Evidence

All identifiers below are anonymized. No personal names, hostnames, channel IDs, session IDs, auth profile IDs, or absolute local paths are included.

### Overflow recovery before patch

A production-like replay showed the context-engine projection repeatedly resuming the same stale native thread:

```text
session=<discord-session>
previousThreadId=<old-native-thread>
projectedPromptChars‚âà222k
native action=resumed thread=<old-native-thread>
```

The app-server then reported auto-compaction success, but the next retry still resumed `<old-native-thread>` instead of starting fresh. The turn eventually exhausted recovery and surfaced the user-facing error:

```text
Auto-compaction could not recover this turn. I kept this conversation mapped to the current session.
```

There was no log evidence that the binding was cleared after the terminal context-window failure, because the overflow happened after `turn/start` had already been accepted.

### Budget compaction before patch

After the first local fix was installed, a Discord-shaped CLI smoke successfully wrote the assistant response:

```text
payload.text = "OK"
provider = openai-codex
action = completed
```

But the CLI still exited non-zero during the post-turn budget lifecycle:

```text
skipping codex app-server compaction for non-manual trigger trigger=budget
CLI native harness compaction did not reduce context
failureReason="codex app-server owns automatic compaction"
```

So the user-visible response succeeded, then the CLI incorrectly converted the intentional Codex app-server budget skip into a failed command.

## After Evidence

### Overflow recovery after patch

With the stale binding restored locally for replay, the same Discord-shaped session no longer reused `<old-native-thread>`. The retry cleared the binding and started a new native thread:

```text
native action=started thread=<new-native-thread>
follow-up native action=resumed thread=<new-native-thread>
```

No `context-overflow-diag`, exhausted recovery, or auto-compaction recovery failure appeared during the replay. The assistant transcript completed with:

```text
payload.text = "OK"
```

### Budget compaction after patch

After adding the CLI-side skip handling and installing this branch into the local runtime, the same smoke command completed successfully:

```text
status = ok
summary = completed
payload.text = "OK"
provider = openai-codex
model = gpt-5.5
exitCode = 0
```

This proves the CLI now preserves the successful turn result when Codex app-server intentionally owns automatic compaction for a budget trigger.

## Follow-up Exact-Route Smoke

A follow-up Discord-shaped replay intentionally used the long session that previously triggered the user-facing failure. The first replay reproduced a failure because the shell CLI was patched, but the LaunchAgent gateway was still resolving a separate global OpenClaw install at `2026.5.27`.

After installing this same PR commit into the gateway-resolved global prefix and restarting the gateway:

```bash
<gateway-openclaw> --version
# OpenClaw 2026.5.28 (3fd6cc0)

curl -sS -m 5 http://127.0.0.1:18789/health
# {"ok":true,"status":"live"}
```

target.title: Add capability manifest verifier plugin
target.body:
## Summary
- Add a bundled `capability-manifest-verifier` extension that registers an OpenClaw trusted tool policy.
- Verify broker-issued HS256 JWT capability manifests from env or a configured local path, with secrets kept out of plugin config.
- Map manifest grants to allow, require approval, or deny decisions; unknown tools fail closed by default.
- Add focused tests for signature validation, expiration/agent checks, approval routing, fail-closed behavior, startup metadata, and metadata discovery.

## Safety posture
- No generic tool passthrough or runtime mutation surface is added.
- Secret material is read only from an environment variable and is not emitted in block reasons.
- Invalid, missing, expired, or mismatched manifests block tool calls when the plugin is enabled.
- The bundled plugin is not enabled by default; operators must explicitly enable/configure it.
- The plugin follows the lightweight bundled-plugin shape: no package dependencies, package manifest, lockfile change, or package-boundary workspace opt-in.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: OpenClaw can discover the bundled `capability-manifest-verifier` extension safely by default, and when explicitly enabled its trusted policy enforces allow, approval, deny, missing-grant, expired-token, and agent-mismatch decisions before tool execution.
- **Real environment tested**: Local macOS OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/worktrees/openclaw-capability-manifest-policy` on branch `evaos-capability-manifest-openclaw`, using the source-built OpenClaw Node runtime and the OpenClaw plugin-test runtime helpers.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - Ran `node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins inspect capability-manifest-verifier --runtime --json` after installing dependencies from the lockfile, then projected the live runtime fields with `jq`.
  - Ran an enabled-policy probe with `node --import tsx --input-type=module`, importing `extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/index.ts` and `openclaw/plugin-sdk/plugin-test-runtime`, signing redacted HS256 manifests in-process, enabling the plugin via `captured.api.pluginConfig`, and evaluating the registered trusted policy against representative tool calls.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the real OpenClaw runtime inspection:

```console
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins inspect capability-manifest-verifier --runtime --json | jq '{id:.plugin.id, origin:.plugin.origin, enabled:.plugin.enabled, activated:.plugin.activated, configSchema:.plugin.configSchema, dependencyStatus:.plugin.dependencyStatus, diagnostics:.diagnostics}'
{
  "id": "capability-manifest-verifier",
  "origin": "bundled",
  "enabled": false,
  "activated": false,
  "configSchema": true,
  "dependencyStatus": {
    "hasDependencies": false,
    "installed": true,
    "requiredInstalled": true,
    "optionalInstalled": true,
    "missing": [],
    "missingOptional": [],
    "dependencies": [],
    "optionalDependencies": []
  },
  "diagnostics": []
}
```

Enabled trusted-policy probe output, with token/secret material redacted and not printed:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <enabled-policy-probe>
{"label":"allow grant","toolName":"desktop_bridge_status","outcome":"allow"}
{"label":"approval grant","toolName":"desktop_bridge_codex_send_visible_message","outcome":"requires_approval","title":"Capability manifest approval required","timeoutBehavior":"deny"}
{"label":"deny grant","toolName":"shell_exec","outcome":"block","reason":"Capability manifest denies tool: shell_exec."}
{"label":"missing grant","toolName":"unknown_tool","outcome":"block","reason":"Capability manifest has no grant for tool: unknown_tool."}
{"label":"expired manifest","toolName":"desktop_bridge_status","outcome":"block","reason":"Capability manifest check failed: manifest expired."}
{"label":"agent mismatch","toolName":"desktop_bridge_status","outcome":"block","reason":"Capability manifest check failed: manifest agent mismatch."}
```

Focused runtime test confirmation for the same verifier surface:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/index.test.ts src/plugins/bundled-plugin-metadata.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts -t "capability-manifest-verifier|trusted tool policy|trusted policy"
Test Files  2 passed | 1 skipped (3)
Tests  18 passed | 78 skipped (96)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The plugin is discoverable as bundled runtime plugin `capability-manifest-verifier`, remains disabled and inactive until explicitly configured, exposes its config schema, reports no missing dependencies, emits no diagnostics, and its enabled trusted policy produces the expected allow, approval, deny, missing, expired, and agent-mismatch outcomes without leaking token or secret material.
- **What was not tested**: Production evaOS broker-issued seeded JWT policy acceptance is intentionally left to the downstream evaOS acceptance path tracked in `electricsheephq/evaos-desktop-bridge#143`; this PR proves the OpenClaw verifier surface and enabled policy behavior in the local source-built runtime.

## Validation
- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/index.test.ts src/plugins/bundled-plugin-metadata.test.ts src/plugins/installed-plugin-index.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.embedded-mode.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/extension-package-project-boundaries.test.ts -t "capability-manifest-verifier|bundled|manifest|metadata|trusted|trusted policy|opt-in extension package boundaries"` -> 7 files, 77 passed, 72 skipped
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/index.ts extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/index.test.ts extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/src/config.ts extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/src/manifest.ts extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/openclaw.plugin.json src/plugins/bundled-plugin-metadata.test.ts .github/labeler.yml`
- `git diff --check`

## evaOS linkage
Part of the evaOS capability-manifest verifier sprint tracked from `electricsheephq/evaos-desktop-bridge#143`.

target.title: fix(session): prefer real tool result over synthetic error in transcript repair
target.body:
## Summary

Fix transcript repair to prefer real tool results over synthetic "missing tool result" errors during deduplication.

When a channel (e.g. Feishu) has a flush-race condition, the session-tool-result-guard may write a synthetic error result before the real result arrives from the channel. On the next session load, `repairToolUseResultPairing` deduplicates by keeping the first-seen result ‚Äî which is the synthetic error. The user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call actually succeeded.

This PR adds `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detection and replaces an existing synthetic result with a later real result during deduplication.

> **Note:** This is a transcript-repair mitigation. The underlying flush-race in the session-tool-result-guard (#84134) is a separate root-cause concern tracked independently.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `session-transcript-repair.ts` | Add `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detector; track output positions; replace synthetic with real during dedup |
| `session-transcript-repair.test.ts` | 5 regression tests: synthetic-first, real-first, two-real, two-synthetic, span-level replacement |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts
 ‚úì unit-fast ../../src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts (40 tests) 5ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  40 passed (40)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a synthetic "missing tool result" error is persisted before the real result arrives (Feishu channel flush race), the repair pass keeps the synthetic error and the user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call succeeded.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, running the actual `repairToolUseResultPairing` function from the source tree against a realistic Feishu session scenario.

### Failing proof before fix (main branch)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on main branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)
  Scenario: Feishu flush race ‚Äî synthetic error persisted before real result arrived

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2
  Kept result isError: true
  Kept result text: "[openclaw] missing tool result in session history; ..."

‚ùå FAIL: Synthetic error was kept
   ‚Üí User sees "Something went wrong" even though message was delivered
```

### Passing proof after fix (this PR)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on fix branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 3
  Tool results: 1
  Kept result isError: false
  Kept result text: "Message sent successfully to Feishu user feishu_user_123"

‚úÖ PASS: Real result preferred over synthetic error
   ‚Üí User sees successful delivery, not "Something went wrong"

--- Scenario 2: real-first, synthetic-second ---
  Kept result isError: false
‚úÖ PASS: Real result kept (synthetic duplicate dropped)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: The repair function correctly identifies synthetic "missing tool result" markers and replaces them with real results when both exist for the same tool call ID. Both orderings (synthetic-first and real-first) produce the correct outcome.
- **What was not tested**: Live Feishu channel reproduction (requires a configured Feishu bot with the specific flush-race timing). The underlying flush race in `session-tool-result-guard` (#84134) is not addressed by this PR ‚Äî it mitigates the downstream effect in the transcript repair pass.

target.title: fix(gateway): strip spurious tool call blocks when provider signals stop
target.body:
## Summary

Strip spurious tool call content blocks from OpenAI-compatible completions streaming responses when the provider signals `finish_reason: "stop"` (not `"tool_calls"`).

## Problem

When an OpenAI-compatible provider (e.g., llama.cpp serving Qwen3.6 with thinking enabled) emits structured `delta.tool_calls` parsed from the model's reasoning/thinking output but correctly reports `finish_reason: "stop"`, the gateway unconditionally builds `toolCall` content blocks and the agent loop executes them.

This is caused by an asymmetric stop-reason guard in `processOpenAICompletionsStream`: the existing guard downgrades `stopReason` from `"toolUse"` to `"stop"` when no tool call blocks are present (line 2679-2681), but the inverse guard ‚Äî stripping tool call blocks when `stopReason` is not `"toolUse"` ‚Äî was missing.

## Fix

Add the missing inverse guard after the existing one:

```typescript
if (hasToolCalls && output.stopReason !== "toolUse") {
  output.content = output.content.filter((block) => block.type !== "toolCall");
}
```

This ensures that when a provider does not signal tool-use intent via `finish_reason`, any tool call blocks in the response are treated as spurious and discarded.

## Tests

Added three test cases to `openai-transport-stream.test.ts`:
1. `delta.tool_calls` + `finish_reason: "stop"` ‚Üí tool call blocks stripped ‚úÖ
2. `delta.tool_calls` + `finish_reason: "tool_calls"` ‚Üí tool calls preserved (regression) ‚úÖ
3. No tool calls + `finish_reason: "stop"` ‚Üí no change (regression) ‚úÖ

All 354 tests pass.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Tool call XML in LLM reasoning/thinking block is executed by gateway when provider signals `finish_reason: "stop"` instead of `"tool_calls"`.
- **Real environment tested**: Native `llama-server` compiled from ggml-org/llama.cpp master (commit `0f3cb3f`), built with `cmake -DGGML_CUDA=ON -DCMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES=80`, running on 3√ó NVIDIA A100-PCIE-40GB. Two models tested: Qwen3-0.6B-Q8_0.gguf (single A100) and Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Q4_K_M.gguf (21GB, 3√ó A100). Thinking mode enabled.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `openai-transport-stream.ts:2678-2681` only has one-way guard (toolUse‚Üístop when no blocks). No inverse guard exists. Source-verified at `openai-transport-stream.ts:2678-2681`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Compiled llama-server from source with CUDA support
  2. Ran 9 test sessions against Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (Q4_K_M) with tool definitions + thinking enabled
  3. Prompted model to execute sequential tool calls (e.g., "Execute sleep 1 thirty times sequentially")
  4. Captured raw SSE streams for each test run
  5. Unit tests: `docker build -f Dockerfile.proof -t openclaw-proof . && docker run --rm openclaw-proof src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**:
  - **Test 8 of 9**: Model generated complete `<tool_call>` XML inside `reasoning_content` while planning tool usage ‚Äî confirming the exact trigger condition from issue #85161. Native llama-server correctly isolated this into `reasoning_content` (not parsed into `delta.tool_calls`), but providers that do not separate reasoning will expose it as spurious tool calls with `finish_reason: "stop"`.
  - **Tests 4, 5**: 35B model produced mixed text `content` + structured `tool_calls` in the same response, demonstrating complex streaming scenarios the guard must handle.
  - **0.6B baseline**: Confirmed `delta.tool_calls` structured output with `finish_reason: "tool_calls"` (correct path) and `reasoning_content` ‚Üí `content` with `finish_reason: "stop"` (text-only path) as two distinct code paths.
  - **Unit tests**: 354 passed (354), including 3 new tests covering the spurious tool call scenario.
  ```
  ‚úì agents-core ../../src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts (177 tests) 184ms
  ‚úì agents-support ../../src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts (177 tests) 152ms
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
       Tests  354 passed (354)
    Duration  4.20s
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The inverse guard `hasToolCalls && stopReason !== "toolUse"` correctly strips spurious tool call blocks when the provider does not signal tool-use intent. This prevents the gateway from executing tool calls that originated in the model's reasoning/thinking output rather than as intentional tool invocations.
- **What was not tested**: End-to-end gateway integration test with a live llama.cpp provider streaming into a running gateway instance. The exact bug scenario (provider that does not separate `reasoning_content` and exposes thinking XML as `delta.tool_calls` with `finish_reason: "stop"`) was confirmed via model behavior analysis and unit test mocks that reproduce the reported SSE structure.

Fixes #85161

target.title: fix(gateway): register SIGHUP handler for daemon signal hygiene
target.body:
## Summary

Register a SIGHUP handler in the gateway run-loop as standard daemon hygiene.

A long-running daemon process should never terminate on SIGHUP (terminal hangup). Previously, the gateway had no SIGHUP listener, so Node.js applied the default OS behavior: terminate with exit code 129. Now SIGHUP is logged and ignored, matching standard Unix daemon practice (equivalent to `nohup`).

SIGTERM and SIGINT remain the supported shutdown signals.

> **Note:** This is defensive hardening. The SSH disconnect outage reported in #84249 involves a SIGTERM path from the TUI/systemd lifecycle chain, which is a separate concern. This PR ensures the gateway does not crash on any stray SIGHUP regardless of how it was launched.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `run-loop.ts` | Add one-line SIGHUP handler: log & ignore |
| `run-loop.test.ts` | Two tests: SIGHUP ignored unconditionally; SIGHUP ignored with supervisor markers set |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts
 ‚úì cli ../../src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts (30 tests) 75ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  30 passed (30)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway process terminates with exit 129 on SIGHUP because no signal handler is registered. Any process that receives a stray SIGHUP (e.g. from a parent terminal closing) would crash.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, OpenClaw `2026.5.18 (50a2481)`.

### Step 1: Verify main branch has no SIGHUP handler

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
(no matches ‚Äî SIGHUP handler is missing on main)
```

### Step 2: Verify fix branch has unconditional SIGHUP ignore

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
127:    process.removeListener("SIGHUP", onSighup);
713:    gatewayLog.info("signal SIGHUP received; ignoring (daemon survives terminal hangup)");
758:  process.on("SIGHUP", onSighup);
```

### Step 3: Real signal delivery ‚Äî process WITH handler (fix)

```
$ node gateway-sighup-test.mjs &
[PID: 98551]

$ kill -HUP 98551
Process still alive ‚úì

$ kill -HUP 98551  # send again
Process still alive after second SIGHUP ‚úì

$ kill -TERM 98551  # clean shutdown
Process exited with code 0 ‚úì (graceful shutdown)
```

### Step 4: Contrast ‚Äî process WITHOUT handler (main branch)

```
$ node gateway-no-handler.mjs &  # no SIGHUP handler
[PID: 98612]

$ kill -HUP 98612
Process terminated with exit code 129 ‚Üê CRASH (default SIGHUP action)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: SIGHUP is unconditionally logged and ignored. Process survives multiple SIGHUP signals and only stops on SIGTERM.
- **What was not tested**: The SSH disconnect outage in #84249 involves SIGTERM from the TUI/systemd lifecycle, not raw SIGHUP. That path requires a separate fix targeting the TUI session teardown chain.

target.title: fix(mcp): kill orphaned stdio children on session teardown + auto-reconnect dead transports
target.body:
## Problem

Two MCP stability issues causing cron failures and mid-turn `Not connected` errors:

1. **Orphaned MCP child processes:** When isolated cron sessions end, the CLI runner only closes the HTTP loopback server but leaves `SessionMcpRuntime` stdio children alive until the idle sweep. The `server-cron.ts` isolated wrapper also only cleans browser sessions. This causes process accumulation (observed 78+ orphaned processes between sweeps).

2. **Unrecoverable transport death:** When an MCP child process dies mid-session (OOM, crash, pipe break), the stdio transport's `send()` throws `Not connected` because `stdin` is gone. This error is unrecoverable for the entire turn, even though the server config is still valid and a fresh process could be spawned.

## Fix

### Commit 1: Kill MCP stdio children on isolated session teardown

- `server-cron.ts`: `runIsolatedAgentJob` finally block now also retires the MCP runtime for isolated cron sessions via `retireSessionMcpRuntimeForSessionKey()`
- `cli-runner.ts`: when `cleanupBundleMcpOnRunEnd` is true, retires MCP runtime by session key (falling back to session ID) after `closeMcpLoopbackServer()`

### Commit 2: Auto-reconnect dead MCP server sessions on tool call failure

- `agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts`: `callTool()` now catches transport-death errors (`Not connected`, `EPIPE`, `ERR_STREAM_DESTROYED`, `write after end`) and attempts a single reconnection: dispose dead session ‚Üí resolve fresh transport ‚Üí connect ‚Üí retry tool call
- If reconnection fails, the original error is thrown unchanged
- Reconnection is logged at warn level for observability

### Commit 3: Separate reconnect failure from retried tool-call error

Split the inner try/catch into two phases per ClawSweeper's [P2] finding:
- **Phase 1** (try/catch): `connectWithTimeout` only. If this fails, dispose the new session and throw the original transport-death error.
- **Phase 2** (unguarded): retry `client.callTool` on the healthy reconnected session. If this fails, it's a normal MCP error from a live server, not a transport issue, so it propagates directly.

This ensures `sessions` map consistency: a healthy reconnected session is never disposed by a subsequent call failure.

### Commit 4: Guard reconnect against McpError server responses

Added `McpError` instanceof guard before the message-substring classifier to prevent misclassifying healthy MCP server errors as transport deaths.

The MCP SDK throws two distinct error types through `client.callTool()`:
- **Transport death:** plain `Error("Not connected")` from `transport.send()` when `this._process?.stdin` is null (child process died)
- **Server errors:** `McpError` instances (extends Error, `.name='McpError'`, `.code`) from JSON-RPC error responses (healthy server, application-level failure)

Before this fix, a healthy Playwright server returning `McpError("Not connected to browser")` would match the `"Not connected"` substring check, causing an unnecessary reconnect and replay of a potentially non-idempotent tool call. Now `McpError` instances short-circuit to `throw` immediately, and only plain `Error` from dead transports enters the reconnect path.

## Real Behavior Proof

### Orphaned MCP child accumulation (before fix)

Live capture from production OpenClaw instance (Mac Studio M1 Max, 32 GB) showing 3 waves of duplicate MCP children under gateway PID 70237, each spawned by successive cron runs without cleanup:

```
=== BEFORE: Current MCP child processes ===
Timestamp: 2026-05-30T00:18:57Z

MCP child process counts by server:
  PID=46292 PPID=70237 RSS=81104KB  ETIME=03:15 proton-pass
  PID=46300 PPID=70237 RSS=117664KB ETIME=03:14 playwright-public
  PID=46301 PPID=70237 RSS=29344KB  ETIME=03:14 cua-driver
  PID=46309 PPID=70237 RSS=116368KB ETIME=03:14 playwright-logged-in
  PID=46316 PPID=70237 RSS=48160KB  ETIME=03:14 threads-mcp
  PID=46317 PPID=70237 RSS=69120KB  ETIME=03:14 veritas

  PID=46336 PPID=70237 RSS=81104KB  ETIME=02:48 proton-pass (wave 2)
  PID=46345 PPID=70237 RSS=116240KB ETIME=02:47 playwright-public
  PID=46346 PPID=70237 RSS=29312KB  ETIME=02:47 cua-driver
  PID=46354 PPID=70237 RSS=115024KB ETIME=02:47 playwright-logged-in
  PID=46361 PPID=70237 RSS=48048KB  ETIME=02:47 threads-mcp
  PID=46362 PPID=70237 RSS=69360KB  ETIME=02:47 veritas

  PID=46410 PPID=70237 RSS=99248KB  ETIME=01:23 proton-pass (wave 3)
  PID=46418 PPID=70237 RSS=147184KB ETIME=01:23 playwright-public
  PID=46419 PPID=70237 RSS=29264KB  ETIME=01:22 cua-driver
  PID=46426 PPID=70237 RSS=135376KB ETIME=01:22 playwright-logged-in
  PID=46433 PPID=70237 RSS=47904KB  ETIME=01:22 threads-mcp
  PID=46434 PPID=70237 RSS=79184KB  ETIME=01:22 veritas

Summary: 6 servers √ó 3 waves = 18 orphaned MCP children, ~1.2 GB RSS
Total MCP-like children: 25 (including non-gateway-parented)
```

Three distinct etime cohorts (03:14, 02:47, 01:22) confirm each cron run spawned a full set of 6 MCP servers and left the previous set alive.

### MCP SDK error type analysis (reconnect classifier proof)

Verified against `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk@1.29.0` (pinned in OpenClaw's package.json):

**Transport death path** (`StdioClientTransport.send()`):
```javascript
// node_modules/@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/dist/esm/client/stdio.js
send(message) {
    return new Promise(resolve => {
        if (!this._process?.stdin) {
            throw new Error('Not connected');  // plain Error, NOT McpError
        }
        // ...
    });
}
```

**Server error path** (`McpError` class):
```javascript
// node_modules/@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/dist/esm/types.js
export class McpError extends Error {
    constructor(code, message, data) {
        super(`MCP error ${code}: ${message}`);
        this.name = 'McpError';  // distinguishable via instanceof
    }
}
```

The `instanceof McpError` guard in commit 4 correctly separates these two paths without relying on fragile message-substring matching for the type distinction.

### Config-level mitigation (already deployed)

In addition to the code fix, 11 cron jobs were updated with `toolsAllow` scoping so they only spawn the MCP servers they actually need. Example: `mcp-orphan-cleanup` only runs a python script via `exec`, so `toolsAllow: ["exec", "process"]` prevents it from spawning 6 MCP servers at all.

### CI failure note

The `checks-node-core-fast` failure is `src/security/audit-sandbox-browser.test.ts` (2 tests timing out at 120s waiting for Docker probes). Unrelated to this PR; no MCP files touched by those tests.

target.title: Feat/browser screenshot vision
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: When the main model is text-only (e.g. `tencent-tokenhub/hy3-preview`), browser screenshots return image content blocks that the model cannot read ‚Äî the agent sees `[image omitted]` and cannot reason about the page.
- Why it matters: Browser-use workflows are unusable with text-only main models; operators must switch to an expensive multimodal main model just to get screenshot readback.
- What changed:
  - The browser screenshot action now optionally runs the captured image through a configured vision model and returns a text description instead of an image block. This is controlled by new fields on the top-level `browser` config namespace (`models`, `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, etc.). When unconfigured, behavior is unchanged.
  - Vision descriptions are wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` (same as snapshot/tabs results) to mitigate prompt injection from untrusted web page content.
  - Local screenshot file size is checked against `maxBytes` before sending to the vision provider.
  - `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` in `src/media-understanding/runtime-types.ts` gains `profile`/`preferredProfile` fields, forwarded through `describeImageFileWithModel` to `describeImage` ‚Äî enabling auth profile selection for all callers of this API, not just the browser tool.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the agent loop, replay-history, session persistence, transport layer, or any other tool. The vision call is entirely contained within the browser extension's screenshot action. The existing `tools.media.image` config and `image_describe` tool are untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: browser-use with text-only models
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A ‚Äî this is a new feature, not a bug fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.test.ts` (20 tests)
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `isBrowserVisionEnabled` returns true/false based on config presence, `enabled: false`, empty models, missing provider/model, CLI-type entries
  - `describeBrowserImageWithVision` calls through to `describeImageFileWithModel` with correct params including prompt, timeout, profile, preferredProfile
  - Local file paths as `mediaUrl` are not forwarded (only HTTP(S) URLs)
  - HTTP(S) `mediaUrl` is correctly forwarded
  - Fallback to next candidate when primary fails
  - Empty model response treated as failure and falls through
  - All candidates failing produces aggregate error with per-candidate details
  - `maxChars` truncation with `[truncated]` marker
  - `maxBytes` enforcement via file stat before vision call
  - `profile`/`preferredProfile` forwarded to `describeImageFileWithModel`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The helper is a pure function with injected deps ‚Äî unit tests cover all branches without needing a real browser or vision API.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `browser-tool.test.ts` mock infrastructure extended to include `describeImageFileWithModel` dep injection.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config fields on the top-level `browser` namespace for vision model configuration:
  ```json
  {
    "browser": {
      "models": [{ "provider": "bytedance", "model": "doubao-seed-2.0-pro" }]
    }
  }
  ```
  Supported fields: `models[]` (with `provider`, `model`, `prompt`, `maxChars`, `maxBytes`, `timeoutSeconds`, `profile`, `preferredProfile`), `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, `visionMaxChars`, `visionMaxBytes`, `visionTimeoutSeconds`.
- When `browser.models` contains at least one valid provider/model entry and `visionEnabled` is not `false`, every browser screenshot is automatically described by the configured vision model. The tool returns a text content block (`[analyzed by <provider>/<model>]\n<wrapped description>`) instead of an image content block. The description text is wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` to guard against prompt injection.
- When `browser.models` is **not** configured, behavior is identical to before ‚Äî screenshots return image content blocks.
- Set `visionEnabled: false` to force-disable vision without removing the model list.
- If vision description fails (API error, timeout, empty response, file exceeds `maxBytes`), the tool falls back to returning the original image block plus a warning text block so the agent loop can still recover.
- `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` now accepts optional `profile`/`preferredProfile` fields. This is a backward-compatible additive change to the media-understanding runtime contract. All existing callers are unaffected; new callers (like browser vision) can pass auth profile selection.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before (text-only main model):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool returns image content block
    -> transport replaces with "[image omitted]"
    -> agent cannot reason about page

After (browser.models configured):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool captures screenshot to disk
    -> stat file size ‚Üí check maxBytes
    -> tool calls vision model (describeImageFileWithModel)
    -> wrapExternalContent(description)
    -> tool returns wrapped text description
    -> text-only main model reads description normally

After (browser.models NOT configured):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool returns image content block (unchanged)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No ‚Äî reuses existing `describeImageFileWithModel` which handles auth through the standard provider registry, SecretRef mechanism, and (now) optional auth profiles.
- New/changed network calls? Yes ‚Äî when `browser.models` is configured, the browser tool makes an additional API call to the configured vision provider to describe the screenshot.
  - Risk: The screenshot image is sent to the configured vision provider's API endpoint.
  - Mitigation: This is opt-in (only when operator explicitly configures `browser.models`). The image data flow is identical to `tools.media.image` / `image_describe` tool ‚Äî same `describeImageFileWithModel` pipeline, same auth, same SSRF guards.
  - Risk: Vision model descriptions contain untrusted web page content that could include prompt injection.
  - Mitigation: All vision description text is wrapped with `wrapExternalContent(text, { source: "browser", includeWarning: true })`, matching the security treatment applied to snapshot and tabs results.
  - Risk: Raw screenshot could be auto-delivered to chat channels if exposed as deliverable media (related: #44759).
  - Mitigation: Addressed ‚Äî the vision-success path does not include any `MEDIA:` directive or `details.media` in the tool result. The raw screenshot file remains on disk for diagnostic purposes only but is not advertised as deliverable media, preventing auto-send of sensitive page content to chat channels.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (darwin)
- Runtime: Node 22, OpenClaw dev build from source (npm link)
- Model/provider: Main model `tencent-tokenhub/hy3-preview` (text-only); vision model `bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro`
- Integration/channel: Control UI webchat
- Relevant config:
  ```json
  {
    "browser": {
      "models": [{ "provider": "bytedance", "model": "doubao-seed-2.0-pro" }]
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure `browser.models` with a vision-capable provider/model
2. Start gateway, open dashboard
3. In a new session, ask the agent to take a browser screenshot (e.g. "open https://example.com and take a screenshot")

### Expected

- Agent receives a text description of the screenshot instead of an image block
- Text-only main model can read and reason about the page content
- Tool result includes `[analyzed by <provider>/<model>]` attribution
- Description text is wrapped in external content markers

### Actual

- Confirmed working: agent received text description, main model reasoned about page content normally

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets: Gateway log shows `[browser/vision] described screenshot via bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro (chars=NNN)` on successful vision call
- [x] Failing before + passing after: Before config, agent sees `[image omitted]`; after config, agent reads full page description
- [x] Unit tests: 20 tests passing in `extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.test.ts`
- [x] CI: `pnpm check:changed` passes (typecheck core/extensions/tests + lint core/extensions + all guards)
- [x] Screenshot/recording

### Video: Vision model describes screenshot for text-only agent

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/32826a25-ca02-4188-9bee-cd7e4bec6639

### Video: Fallback sanitization (new, current HEAD)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d7051b53-6935-4f8c-8899-b60fc55ce72d

Source-level evidence for the image content block that this fallback path also returns (the binary `image` content block itself is not easy to film in webchat, so it is shown via the gateway log line emitted by `imageResultFromFile` ‚Üí `resolveRuntimeImageSanitization` ‚Üí sharp resize):

```
2026-05-21T09:11:23.579+08:00 [browser/vision] candidate fail-on-purpose-for-fallback-demo/no-such-model failed: image description timed out after 2000ms
2026-05-21T09:11:23.614+08:00 [agents/tool-images] Image resized to fit limits: 2000x1140px 74.8KB -> 9.2KB (-87.7%)
```

Interpretation:
- Line 1 = the vision provider call rejected/timed out, so the screenshot tool entered the `catch` fallback at `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.ts:833`.
- Line 2 = the fallback called `imageResultFromFile({ ..., imageSanitization: resolveRuntimeImageSanitization() })` with `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx: 512`, and the upstream image-result helper resized the raw 2000√ó1140 screenshot before the image content block was added to the tool result. Without the P2 fix, this `imageSanitization` argument was absent, so the original 2000√ó1140 image would have been returned unscaled.

Reproduction config used for the video:

```jsonc
{
  "agents": { "defaults": { "imageMaxDimensionPx": 512 } },
  "browser": {
    "models": [
      { "provider": "fail-on-purpose-for-fallback-demo", "model": "no-such-model", "timeoutSeconds": 2 }
    ]
  }
}
```

A non-existent vision provider id is used so the failure is deterministic and does not depend on any external network call. `visionEnabled` defaults to enabled whenever `browser.models[]` resolves at least one valid candidate, and `visionTimeoutSeconds` is only consulted as the fallback when an individual `models[].timeoutSeconds` is unset, so both top-level fields are omitted here.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Text-only main models can now read and reason about browser screenshots via a configured vision model, instead of receiving `[image omitted]`.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.3 arm64, Node v25.8.1, OpenClaw built from source (`pnpm build` ‚Üí `node dist/index.js gateway --port 18789`), main model hy3-preview (tencent-tokenhub, text-only), vision model doubao-seed-2.0-pro (bytedance), webchat dashboard.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Built source: `pnpm build`
  2. Started gateway from source: `node dist/index.js gateway --port 18789`
  3. Configured `browser.models` with `bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro`
  4. Opened webchat dashboard, asked agent to screenshot a Discord page
  5. Agent captured screenshot ‚Üí vision model described it ‚Üí agent reasoned about page content and responded with a text summary
- Evidence after fix: Screen recording showing the full flow: agent takes screenshot, vision model processes it, agent returns a coherent text description of the page content. See "Video: After" above.
- Observed result after fix: Tool result contains `[analyzed by bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro]` attribution + wrapped text description. Main model (hy3-preview) successfully reads and reasons about the Discord channel content.
- What was not tested: Remote/node browser proxy screenshots; multiple fallback candidates in sequence (only single-candidate tested live); per-agent auth profile selection.
- Fresh ClawSweeper-flagged proof at the current HEAD: scenario (b) "vision-failure fallback with `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` configured ‚Äî confirming the returned image block is downscaled by the sanitization guard" is captured by the **Video: Fallback sanitization** above plus the cited `[agents/tool-images] Image resized to fit limits: 2000x1140px 74.8KB -> 9.2KB` gateway log line (the raw `image` content block itself is not visible in webchat UI, hence the log-based supplement). Scenario (a) "directive-shaped `MEDIA:/tmp/secret.png` in page or vision output does not synthesize media delivery" is covered by the unit + integration regression tests landed in this PR (`extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.test.ts` and `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts`).

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Text-only main model + browser screenshot with `browser.models` configured ‚Üí text description returned, agent reasons about page
  - Vision call failure ‚Üí fallback to image block + warning text
  - No `browser.models` configured ‚Üí original image block behavior unchanged
  - Vision text containing `MEDIA:` directive line ‚Üí neutralized in tool-result text, `details.media` absent (regression test)
  - Vision failure fallback with `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` ‚Üí fallback `imageResultFromFile` receives matching `imageSanitization` (regression test)
- Edge cases checked:
  - Local file path passed as `mediaUrl` ‚Äî fixed: only forward HTTP(S) URLs to describeImageFileWithModel
  - Provider mismatch (`agent-plan` vs `bytedance`) ‚Äî learned that provider id must match the media-understanding registry, same as `tools.media.image`
  - `wrapExternalContent` applied to vision output ‚Äî verified in code review
  - `maxBytes` file stat guard ‚Äî verified via unit test
  - `profile`/`preferredProfile` forwarding ‚Äî verified end-to-end through runtime-types ‚Üí runtime.ts ‚Üí describeImage
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Remote/node browser proxy screenshots (only tested host browser)
  - Multiple fallback candidates in sequence (only single-candidate config tested live; unit test covers this path)
  - Per-agent auth profile selection (see Known Limitations)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

### ClawSweeper findings addressed

Previous review round:

- **[P1] Do not advertise vision screenshots as deliverable media** ‚Äî Fixed: the vision-success path no longer includes any `MEDIA:` directive or `details.media` in the tool result. Raw screenshots are not auto-delivered to chat channels. Related: #44759.
- **[P2] Remove the unused schema import** ‚Äî Fixed: removed orphaned `ToolsMediaUnderstandingSchema` import from `zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts`.

Latest review round:

- **[P1] Neutralize media directives in vision text** (`extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.ts:801-807`) ‚Äî Fixed: introduced `neutralizeMediaDirectives` in `extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.ts`. The vision-success path now runs `described.text.trim()` through this helper *before* `wrapExternalContent`, inserting `[neutralized] ` in front of every line whose `trimStart()` begins with `MEDIA:` (case-insensitive). `wrapExternalContent` itself does not strip line-start directives and the agent media extractor (`splitMediaFromOutput`) scans every browser tool-result text block; the `browser` tool is on the trusted-media allowlist, so this guard is required to prevent untrusted page or vision-provider output (e.g. `MEDIA:/tmp/secret.png`) from synthesizing channel-deliverable media artifacts. Regression covered:
  - Unit tests in `extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.test.ts` for the helper itself (no-op fast path, mid-line `MEDIA:` is not defanged, line-start with arbitrary leading whitespace is defanged, case-insensitive, multiple directives in one blob).
  - Integration test in `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts` (`neutralizes MEDIA: directives in vision text and does not attach media`) that drives the full `screenshot` tool execution with a vision-provider response containing `MEDIA:/tmp/secret.png` and asserts: no text-content line in the result matches `/^\s*MEDIA:/i`, the secret path text is still preserved verbatim (defanged not deleted), `details.media` is absent, and `imageResultFromFile` is never called on the success path.

- **[P2] Keep image sanitization on vision failure fallback** (`extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.ts:828-833`) ‚Äî Fixed: the vision-failure catch block now passes `imageSanitization: resolveRuntimeImageSanitization()` to `imageResultFromFile`, matching what the non-vision screenshot path at the same callsite already supplies. Without this, any provider error (timeout, auth failure, oversized file) silently bypasses the existing `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` guard added in `d5cc0d53b7e3`. Regression covered:
  - Integration test in `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts` (`preserves screenshot image sanitization on vision failure fallback`) that configures `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx: 1600`, forces `describeImageFileWithModel` to reject, and asserts the fallback `imageResultFromFile` call receives `imageSanitization: { maxDimensionPx: 1600 }` along with the failure `extraText`.



## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes ‚Äî no behavior change without explicit `browser.models` config.
- Config/env changes? Yes ‚Äî new optional fields on the top-level `browser` config namespace (`models`, `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, `visionMaxChars`, `visionMaxBytes`, `visionTimeoutSeconds`).
- Migration needed? No ‚Äî purely additive.
- `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` gains two optional fields (`profile`, `preferredProfile`) ‚Äî backward compatible, no existing callers affected.

## Known Limitations

- **scope/attachments fields not implemented**: The `tools.media.image` config shape includes `scope` and `attachments` fields, but these are not exposed for `browser` because the browser tool's vision is always triggered on the just-captured screenshot ‚Äî there is no attachment selection or scope gating semantic. These can be added in a follow-up if a use case emerges.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Vision API latency adds to screenshot tool response time (typically 5‚Äì25s depending on provider).
  - Mitigation: Configurable `timeoutSeconds`; on failure, falls back to returning the raw image block so the agent loop is never blocked.
- Risk: Operator configures wrong provider id (e.g. `agent-plan` instead of `bytedance`) and gets silent failures.
  - Mitigation: Error message surfaces the provider/model that failed; the config shape mirrors `tools.media.image` so operators can copy working config directly.
- Risk: Oversized screenshots sent to vision providers.
  - Mitigation: File size checked against `maxBytes` (default 10 MB) before vision call; oversized files skip the candidate with a logged warning.
- Risk: Web page content in vision descriptions could contain prompt injection.
  - Mitigation: Vision output wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` with warning, matching existing snapshot/tabs treatment.

target.title: Keep core doctor health in contribution order
target.body:
## Summary

This PR integrates structured health checks with the ordered core doctor checks. The goal is to make it cheap and safe to add structured findings, dry-run previews, and diff/effect reporting to existing doctor rules without moving those rules out of the doctor order that already works.

The model is:

- core doctor checks stay owned by `resolveDoctorHealthContributions()` / `createDoctorHealthContribution(...)`, which remains the source of truth for `openclaw doctor` and `openclaw doctor --fix` ordering
- core contributions can now declare `healthChecks` directly; for a single inline check the factory derives `id`, `kind: "core"`, `source: "doctor"`, and `healthCheckIds`
- if a contribution still has legacy `run`, that legacy path remains authoritative for normal `doctor` and real `doctor --fix` mutation
- while legacy `run` is still present, the structured health check should usually add `detect`; any structured `repair` / structured `run` should be preview-only for dry-run, diff, and effect reporting
- the full switchover only happens when legacy `run` is deliberately removed; then the old repair behavior is called from the structured health check and `healthChecks` owns the contribution execution path
- `doctor --lint` runs structured core checks in contribution order first, then appends non-core bundled/plugin registry checks
- the shared health registry is now the extension registry surface: bundled/plugin checks register there and run after the ordered core contribution checks
- core doctor checks do not register into that shared registry for ordering; their order comes from the doctor contribution list
- the broad structured repair bridge is reserved for extension/plugin registry checks rather than becoming a second core repair runner
- the temporary doctor health conversion-plan ledger is removed because contribution ordering and contribution/core-check tests now own the invariant directly

That gives us the migration ladder we wanted: keep existing doctor repair behavior in place while adding structured lint/dry-run/diff data, then move the old repair behavior behind `healthChecks` only when that specific core contribution is ready for full structured ownership. It also keeps the ownership boundary clear: core doctor ordering lives in contributions, while the shared health registry remains the extension point for bundled/plugin checks.


## Concrete Example: #85566

#85566 is the first small rule migration that shows why this PR exists.

The merged bridge shape registered `core/doctor/shell-completion` as structured
health while keeping real repair in the existing positional Doctor path. That
was the safe first step: lint/dry-run data could exist without moving real
`doctor --fix` behavior.

This PR makes the target shape available for follow-up migrations:

```ts
createDoctorHealthContribution({
  id: "doctor:shell-completion",
  label: "Shell completion",
  healthChecks: {
    id: "core/doctor/shell-completion",
    description: "Shell completion profile and cache are ready.",
    async run(ctx, scope) {
      return runShellCompletionHealth(ctx, scope);
    },
  },
  run: runLegacyShellCompletionDoctorRepair,
})
```

That lets `doctor --lint` report `core/doctor/shell-completion` through the
ordered core contribution path, while `openclaw doctor --fix` can keep the
legacy shell-completion repair path in its original Doctor slot until that rule
intentionally moves fully behind `healthChecks`.

Merged bridge example: #85566, merge commit
dc17412c3a9557f16a993a35286d3bb9a4d4e42d.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
This PR changes the doctor health execution boundary: ordered core doctor
contributions supply core lint checks first, while bundled/plugin registry
checks remain the extension surface after core checks.

Real environment tested:
WSL Ubuntu 24.04 durable OpenClaw worktree at
`/root/src/openclaw-86627-core-order`, rebased signed head `cd16c6b6401`,
isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, `HOME`, and workspace
under `/tmp/openclaw-86627-proof-refresh-node`.

Proof 1: real command-path ordering with a registered non-core health check.
The proof enabled the bundled Policy plugin, then used the same sequence as
`doctor --lint`: read config, register bundled health checks, resolve ordered
core contribution checks, then append non-core registry checks.

Output:

```json
{"orderedSubset":["core/doctor/gateway-config","core/doctor/command-owner","policy/policy-jsonc-missing"],"coreCount":19,"extensionCount":52}
```

This shows the registered non-core Policy check is appended after the ordered
core Doctor checks.

Proof 2: real source-entry `doctor --lint --json` with selected core and
registered non-core checks.

Command shape:

```bash
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-86627-proof-refresh-node/state \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-86627-proof-refresh-node/state/openclaw.json \
HOME=/tmp/openclaw-86627-proof-refresh-node/home \
CI=1 TERM=dumb \
node --import tsx src/entry.ts doctor --lint --json \
  --only core/doctor/gateway-config \
  --only core/doctor/command-owner \
  --only policy/policy-jsonc-missing
```

Output:

```json
{"ok":false,"checksRun":3,"checksSkipped":68,"findings":[{"checkId":"core/doctor/gateway-config","severity":"warning","message":"gateway.mode is unset; gateway start will be blocked.","path":"gateway.mode","fixHint":"Run `openclaw configure` and set Gateway mode (local/remote), or `openclaw config set gateway.mode local`."},{"checkId":"policy/policy-jsonc-missing","severity":"warning","message":"policy.jsonc is missing for the enabled Policy plugin.","source":"policy","path":"policy.jsonc","fixHint":"Restore policy.jsonc or add the policy artifact for this workspace."},{"checkId":"core/doctor/command-owner","severity":"info","message":"No command owner is configured. Owner-only commands (/diagnostics, /export-trajectory, /config, exec approvals) have no allowed sender.","path":"commands.ownerAllowFrom","fixHint":"Set commands.ownerAllowFrom to your channel user id, e.g. `openclaw config set commands.ownerAllowFrom '[\"telegram:123456789\"]'`."}]}
```

Exit code: `1`, expected because the selected checks intentionally report
findings.

What was not tested:
No plugin package was installed. The non-core registry proof uses the bundled
Policy plugin as the registered extension-health surface.

## How A Core Rule Upgrades

Before an upgrade, a rule is just an ordered doctor contribution:

```ts
createDoctorHealthContribution({
  id: "doctor:some-rule",
  label: "Some rule",
  run: runSomeRuleHealth,
})
```

For the safe in-place upgrade, the rule keeps its old `run` and adds one inline health check beside it. Legacy `run` still owns real doctor behavior; `healthChecks.detect` supplies structured lint findings:

```ts
createDoctorHealthContribution({
  id: "doctor:some-rule",
  label: "Some rule",
  run: runSomeRuleHealth,
  healthChecks: {
    description: "Some rule is valid.",
    async detect(ctx) {
      return detectSomeRuleFindings(ctx);
    },
  },
})
```

If the rule can provide dry-run/diff/effect detail before the full switchover, it can add structured repair as a preview-only path while legacy `run` still owns real mutation:

```ts
createDoctorHealthContribution({
  id: "doctor:some-rule",
  label: "Some rule",
  run: runSomeRuleHealth,
  healthChecks: {
    description: "Some rule is valid.",
    async detect(ctx) {
      return detectSomeRuleFindings(ctx);
    },
    async repair(ctx, findings) {
      return previewSomeRuleRepair(ctx, findings);
    },
  },
})
```

When the rule is ready to be fully structured, remove legacy `run` and call the old repair behavior from the structured check. At that point `healthChecks` owns the contribution execution path:

```ts
createDoctorHealthContribution({
  id: "doctor:some-rule",
  label: "Some rule",
  healthChecks: {
    description: "Some rule is valid.",
    async run(ctx, scope) {
      const findings = await detectSomeRuleFindings(ctx, scope);
      if (!ctx.repair && !ctx.previewRepair) {
        return { findings };
      }
      if (ctx.previewRepair) {
        return previewSomeRuleRepair(ctx, findings);
      }
      return runSomeRuleRepairAsStructured(ctx, findings);
    },
  },
})
```

When one fully structured contribution owns multiple structured checks, each check names its own ID explicitly:

```ts
createDoctorHealthContribution({
  id: "doctor:browser",
  label: "Browser",
  healthChecks: [
    {
      id: "core/doctor/browser",
      description: "Browser readiness is valid.",
      async detect(ctx) {
        return collectBrowserReadinessFindings(ctx);
      },
    },
    {
      id: "core/doctor/browser-clawd-profile-residue",
      description: "Legacy browser profile residue is absent.",
      async detect(ctx) {
        return detectBrowserResidueFindings(ctx);
      },
      async repair(ctx, findings) {
        return previewOrArchiveBrowserResidue(ctx, findings);
      },
    },
  ],
})
```

If a check has repair behavior, it is expected to honor the existing dry-run/diff/effects conventions.

## Validation

- Rebased onto `origin/main` at `e9dee8dfe15`; resolved doctor conflicts by
  preserving this PR's contribution-owned core ordering and current main's
  Gateway SecretRef `--allow-exec` guards, `allowExecSecretRefs`, auth
  profile `api_key` alias repair, skipped Gateway health forwarding,
  runtime tool schema, heartbeat template, and read-only model-catalog
  behavior.
- `git diff --check`
- `node node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/commands/doctor-lint.ts src/commands/doctor-lint.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.ts docs/cli/doctor.md`
- `node node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/commands/doctor-lint.ts src/commands/doctor-lint.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/commands/doctor-gateway-auth-token.test.ts src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts src/commands/doctor-gateway-daemon-flow.test.ts src/commands/doctor-lint.test.ts --reporter=dot --pool=forks --testTimeout=30000 --hookTimeout=30000` (5 files, 109 tests)
- Added focused regression coverage that the contribution-owned paths preserve Gateway auth `--allow-exec` SecretRef resolution, forward `allowExecSecretRefs` to Gateway service repair, skip Gateway health probes for exec SecretRefs unless `--allow-exec` is set, keep auth-profile `api_key` alias repair wired, and forward skipped Gateway health to daemon repair.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental false --pretty false`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit --incremental false --pretty false`
- Rebased signed head: `cd16c6b6401`.

## Updated In-Place Upgrade Stack

These are the doctor PRs in the in-place upgrade series. Each one keeps the existing contribution order and preserves legacy real repair unless that PR explicitly removes the legacy run path.

| PR | Area | Shape |
| --- | --- | --- |
| #85566 | Shell completion | Structured lint health is owned by the existing doctor:shell-completion contribution. |
| #84290 | UI freshness | Structured lint/dry-run effects report missing or stale Control UI assets, while real build/rebuild stays on the existing doctor UI freshness path. |
| #84366 | Session locks | Structured lint/dry-run effects report stale lock cleanup, while real cleanup stays on the existing session-lock doctor path. |
| #84450 | Config audit scrub | Next small config/state slice: structured lint/dry-run effects report historical config-audit argv redaction cleanup, while real scrub stays on the existing config-audit doctor path. |
| #84326 | Sandbox registry | Structured lint/dry-run effects report legacy sandbox registry migrate/remove/quarantine work, while real migration stays on the existing sandbox doctor path. |
| #84340 | Gateway extra services | Structured lint/dry-run effects report extra gateway-like services and legacy cleanup previews, while real cleanup stays on the existing gateway-services doctor path. |

The remaining older doctor repair drafts should be rebased onto this PR and reduced to this same in-place shape before they are presented as part of the series.

target.title: fix(messages): use best-effort for implicit tool-only source replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Force implicit and explicit-current-source `message_tool_only` replies through best-effort delivery before target normalization.
- Preserve required durable delivery when the model supplies an explicit external message target with `bestEffort: false`.
- Add regression coverage for the Slack-style adapter path that lacks `reconcileUnknownSend`.

## Root Cause

For Slack group/channel source replies with visible replies routed through the `message` tool, a source-channel reply could still carry `bestEffort: false`, including the case where the tool explicitly supplied the current source channel as `target`. That made the outbound runner request required durable delivery, which then failed preflight on Slack because required durable sends need `reconcileUnknownSend`.

Current-source replies are user-visible conversational replies, not explicit cross-channel durable sends, so they should use best-effort delivery even when the target equals the current source conversation. Explicit external targeted sends keep the existing required durable semantics.

Fixes #84078.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `message_tool_only` visible source replies that omit an explicit target, or explicitly target the current source Slack channel, no longer fail the durable-send preflight when Slack lacks `reconcileUnknownSend`; explicit external targeted sends still keep required durable fail-closed behavior.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at `aff69bbcc622`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.15.0`, OpenClaw runtime loaded from this branch, real Slack channel adapter, real Slack Web API delivery into a dedicated test channel. Tokens, workspace URL, channel name, and full channel id are redacted.
- **Exact proof route**: `runMessageAction -> core outbound -> real Slack adapter -> Slack Web API`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node --import tsx --input-type=module <real-slack-source-reply-proof>` with the active plugin registry set to the real Slack channel plugin, `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"`, `toolContext.currentChannelProvider: "slack"`, and `toolContext.currentChannelId` set to the same redacted test channel for the two source-reply cases.
- **Evidence after fix**:

```json
{
  "status": "PASS",
  "pr": 84232,
  "branch": "fix/slack-message-tool-durable-fallback",
  "head": "aff69bbcc622",
  "route": "runMessageAction -> core outbound -> real Slack adapter -> Slack Web API",
  "channel": {
    "id": "C0B5...B7T",
    "name": "[redacted-test-channel]"
  },
  "marker": "OC84232_1779243865565",
  "sends": [
    {
      "name": "omitted-target-source-reply",
      "kind": "send",
      "channel": "slack",
      "to": "[redacted-test-channel]",
      "handledBy": "core",
      "messageId": "1779243866.436879",
      "via": "direct"
    },
    {
      "name": "explicit-current-source-target",
      "kind": "send",
      "channel": "slack",
      "to": "[redacted-test-channel]",
      "handledBy": "core",
      "messageId": "1779243867.013869",
      "via": "direct"
    }
  ],
  "externalFailure": {
    "name": "external-required-durable",
    "failedClosed": true,
    "error": "Required durable message send is unsupported for slack: missing reconcileUnknownSend"
  },
  "observedCount": 2,
  "observed": [
    {
      "ts": "1779243866.436879",
      "text": "OC84232_1779243865565 omitted target source reply",
      "bot_id": "[present]",
      "user": "[present]"
    },
    {
      "ts": "1779243867.013869",
      "text": "OC84232_1779243865565 explicit current-source target reply",
      "bot_id": "[present]",
      "user": "[present]"
    }
  ]
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: Both current-source `message_tool_only` sends completed through the core outbound path and posted to a real Slack test channel even when the input carried `bestEffort: false`: one omitted the target and one explicitly used the current Slack source channel as `target`. The explicit external targeted send with `bestEffort: false` failed before delivery with `missing reconcileUnknownSend`, preserving the required durable guard for non-source targets.
- **What was not tested**: This proof did not run a full live LLM/gateway Slack inbound turn. It specifically validates the patched message-tool send path with the real Slack adapter and real Slack delivery, replacing the earlier local Slack-shaped adapter proof.

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.core-send.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.send-validation.test.ts src/infra/outbound/outbound-send-service.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`

commands.command: re-review, my preference would be a slack plugin-owned contract rather than a raw slack api escape hatch, since that seems more maintainable and fits the existing modal submission/close routing. a workflow-facing api like this would cover the use case well: - `slack.modal.open` - `slack.modal.update` - `slack.modal.close` with support for: - block kit `view` payloads - `callback_id` - `private_metadata` - routing `view_submission` / `view_closed` back to the owning workflow/plugin - returning slack validation errors from submission handlers - clear docs around ownership, lifecycle, and callback routing i agree the key decision is the ownership boundary. from a user/workflow perspective, the important part is that workflows can initiate the modal lifecycle, not only receive modal callbacks from modals created elsewhere.
commands.freeform_prompt: re-review, My preference would be a Slack plugin-owned contract rather than a raw Slack API escape hatch, since that seems more maintainable and fits the existing modal submission/close routing. A workflow-facing API like this would cover the use case well: - `slack.modal.open` - `slack.modal.update` - `slack.modal.close` With support for: - Block Kit `view` payloads - `callback_id` - `private_metadata` - routing `view_submission` / `view_closed` back to the owning workflow/plugin - returning Slack validation errors from submission handlers - clear docs around ownership, lifecycle, and callback routing I agree the key decision is the ownership boundary. From a user/workflow perspective, the important part is that workflows can initiate the modal lifecycle, not only receive modal callbacks from modals created elsewhere.

target.title: [Feature]: Add Slack Modal Support for Interactive Workflows
target.author: jhabers

commands.reason: author association NONE and repository permission read are not allowed

commands.author: jhabers
commands.command: re-review, to clarify the request: i'm not reporting missing modal callback handling. i'm asking for a first-class workflow/plugin surface that can *open, update, close, and validate* slack modals from openclaw workflows, while routing `view_submission` and `view_closed` payloads back into the workflow. expected behavior: - a workflow/plugin can call a supported slack modal action to open a modal using block kit. - the workflow can update or close the modal when needed. - submitted modal values are exposed back to the workflow in a structured way. - validation errors can be returned to slack on submission. - modal ownership and routing are documented clearly. actual behavior: - current slack support appears to handle modal submissions/closes for openclaw-routed or plugin-owned modals. - there does not appear to be a documented first-class workflow action/api for opening, updating, closing, or validating slack modals. i agree the main implementation question is the ownership boundary: whether modals should be owned by the slack plugin, individual plugins, workflows, or some shared interaction layer. a reasonable direction might be: - slack plugin exposes generic modal primitives: `open`, `update`, `close`. - workflows/plugins provide block kit views plus a `callback_id` / metadata. - slack plugin routes `view_submission` / `view_closed` events back to the owning workflow/plugin. - validation responses are supported for `view_submission`. i don't have a broken-behavior screenshot because this is a missing feature/workflow surface rather than an existing ui failure.
commands.freeform_prompt: re-review, To clarify the request: I'm not reporting missing modal callback handling. I'm asking for a first-class workflow/plugin surface that can *open, update, close, and validate* Slack modals from OpenClaw workflows, while routing `view_submission` and `view_closed` payloads back into the workflow. Expected behavior: - A workflow/plugin can call a supported Slack modal action to open a modal using Block Kit. - The workflow can update or close the modal when needed. - Submitted modal values are exposed back to the workflow in a structured way. - Validation errors can be returned to Slack on submission. - Modal ownership and routing are documented clearly. Actual behavior: - Current Slack support appears to handle modal submissions/closes for OpenClaw-routed or plugin-owned modals. - There does not appear to be a documented first-class workflow action/API for opening, updating, closing, or validating Slack modals. I agree the main implementation question is the ownership boundary: whether modals should be owned by the Slack plugin, individual plugins, workflows, or some shared interaction layer. A reasonable direction might be: - Slack plugin exposes generic modal primitives: `open`, `update`, `close`. - Workflows/plugins provide Block Kit views plus a `callback_id` / metadata. - Slack plugin routes `view_submission` / `view_closed` events back to the owning workflow/plugin. - Validation responses are supported for `view_submission`. I don't have a broken-behavior screenshot because this is a missing feature/workflow surface rather than an existing UI failure.

target.title: Fix restart sentinel internal continuations
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #87792.

This makes restart-sentinel continuations internal by default instead of letting synthetic restart turns auto-deliver final assistant output back to the originating channel. It also preserves current channel/thread/message context for CLI, MCP, and queued follow-up paths so an explicit post-restart continuation can still speak only when it deliberately uses the message tool.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`: `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`
- `pnpm build`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/restart-sentinel.test.ts src/gateway/server-restart-sentinel.test.ts src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session-restart.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.test.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts src/gateway/tool-resolution.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts test/scripts/label-open-issues.test.ts test/scripts/gh-read.test.ts src/security/audit-sandbox-browser.test.ts`: passed, 23 files / 541 tests
- Blacksmith Testbox regression proof: provider `blacksmith-testbox`, focused build + regression suite passed, 23 files / 541 tests
- Local live WhatsApp proof with Convex-leased `whatsapp` QA credential and mock-openai provider mode: passed; credential identifier redacted

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: Restart-sentinel `agentTurn` continuations no longer auto-deliver plain final assistant output into the originating channel. The restart notice itself still delivers to the live channel route.

Real environment tested: Local source checkout with the real WhatsApp transport, a Convex-leased `whatsapp` QA credential, and mock-openai provider mode. Credential identifiers and live routing details are redacted from this PR body.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Built the current branch, started the QA WhatsApp driver and SUT gateway, sent a real WhatsApp group seed turn, wrote a restart sentinel with an explicit `agentTurn` continuation marker, restarted the gateway via `restartAfterStateMutation`, then watched the live group for the restart notice and for absence of the continuation marker.

Evidence after fix: Local live proof passed with `ISSUE_87792_LIVE_FIX_PROOF pass credential=<redacted> seedMs=5182 noticeMs=10379` and `ISSUE_87792_LIVE_REGRESSION_REPRO pass continuationMarkerAbsentMs=30000`.

Observed result after fix: The seed WhatsApp group turn replied, the restart notice was delivered after restart, and the explicit continuation final marker did not appear in the live WhatsApp group during the regression window.

What was not tested: I did not run a live Telegram or Discord restart-sentinel path. Blacksmith could run the focused regression suite, but the exact custom live proof could not run there because the delegated Testbox path cannot forward local credential env; a workflow-backed WhatsApp live lane was blocked by a logged-out pooled WhatsApp session before scenario execution.
